channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: On 13 April 2029, a 375-metre asteroid called Apophis will sweep past Earth closer than many telecommunications satellites — and, under clear dark skies, around two billion people across Europe, Africa and parts of Asia may be able to watch it move across t - Space Daily
item.description:

item.title: 50 Years Later, NASA's Voyager Probe Is Reaching A Big Milestone For Space Travel - bgr.com
item.description:
1. 50 Years Later, NASA's Voyager Probe Is Reaching A Big Milestone For Space Travel  bgr.com
  2. The Voyager 1 probe, launched in 1977, is now so far from Earth that a radio signal travelling at the speed of light takes over 22 hours to reach it, and it is still sending data back from interstellar space on a power source the size of a car battery  Space Daily
  3. NASA Has Not Launched Anything Faster Than Voyager in 48 Years. Here Is the Real Menu of Machines That Could Finally Beat It  19FortyFive
  4. In 2024, engineers rescued Voyager 1 after it spent months transmitting gibberish, remotely rewriting and relocating pieces of 46-year-old code around a failed memory chip from more than 24 billion kilometres away.  Space Daily
  5. NASA’s Voyager 1 left Earth in 1977; now its signal takes over 22 hours to reach us and it is still sending data  The Times of India

item.title: For 21 years, Voyager 1’s primary roll thrusters were considered dead. Then in 2025, NASA tried to wake them up from 15 billion miles away — knowing that if they fired cold, they could explode, but if they stayed dead, the spacecraft might one day lose its aim - Space Daily
item.description: For 21 years, Voyager 1’s primary roll thrusters were considered dead. Then in 2025, NASA tried to wake them up from 15 billion miles away — knowing that if they fired cold, they could explode, but if they stayed dead, the spacecraft might one day lose its aim  Space Daily

  4. 🔭 The uniform universe? A fundamental principle of cosmology disproven by observation  Techno-Science.net

item.title: Chinese scientists find the best way to nuke an asteroid on its way to impact Earth - Space
item.description: Chinese scientists find the best way to nuke an asteroid on its way to impact Earth  SpaceSee more headlines & perspectives on Google News

item.title: We tend to think of Jupiter as permanently giant, but a 2025 study found it used to be twice its current size, with a magnetic field 50 times more powerful, and it is still quietly shrinking by about 2 centimetres every year - Space Daily
item.description: We tend to think of Jupiter as permanently giant, but a 2025 study found it used to be twice its current size, with a magnetic field 50 times more powerful, and it is still quietly shrinking by about 2 centimetres every year  Space Daily

item.title: A probe called Cassini spent thirteen years orbiting Saturn, flew through the plumes of its moon Enceladus, tasted water and organic molecules in the spray, and then was deliberately crashed into Saturn's atmosphere in 2017 so it could not contaminate what - Space Daily
item.description:
1. A probe called Cassini spent thirteen years orbiting Saturn, flew through the plumes of its moon Enceladus, tasted water and organic molecules in the spray, and then was deliberately crashed into Saturn's atmosphere in 2017 so it could not contaminate what  Space Daily
  2. For The First Time, Scientists 'Heard' Plasma Waves Passing Between Saturn And Enceladus  ScienceAlert
  3. In 2008, Cassini skimmed past Enceladus’s south pole and flew straight through a plume of icy material erupting from cracks in the moon’s surface. The encounter lasted only minutes, but nearly two decades later, data from that flyby is still revealing new organ  Space Daily

item.title: Chemo-Free Technique Destroys 99% of Cancer Cells in The Lab - ScienceAlert
item.description: Chemo-Free Technique Destroys 99% of Cancer Cells in The Lab  ScienceAlert

item.title: People Who Can’t Visualize Anything Are Challenging a 300-Year-Old Theory of Thought - Gizmodo
item.description:
1. People Who Can’t Visualize Anything Are Challenging a 300-Year-Old Theory of Thought  Gizmodo
  2. Can your child picture an apple? The answer may affect how they learn at school  The Independent
  3. Imagination, aphantasia and what it reveals about AI audiences  Campaign
  4. Aphantasia Shatters Classical Theories of Imagistic Thought  Neuroscience News
  5. Aphantasia challenges a centuries-old theory of abstract thought  Medical Xpress

item.title: How a chance discovery overturned one of photochemistry’s oldest dogmas - Chemistry World
item.description: How a chance discovery overturned one of photochemistry’s oldest dogmas  Chemistry World
